message_header.from_name_and_position: Daniel Alvarez, Shelter Operations Unit Leader
message_header.subject: Request for additional cots, blankets, and pediatric supplies for Riverbend High School shelter

message_content.message:
LOCATION & CONTEXT:
Shelter: Riverbend High School, 1420 Willow Creek Rd, Riverbend, OH 44127
Shelter Operations Contact: Daniel Alvarez, Shelter Operations Unit Leader
Cell: (216) 555-3498 | Email: dalvarez.ops@riverbendoh.gov

SITUATION SUMMARY (AS OF 2025-12-11 21:15):
The Riverbend High School shelter has been operating since 2025-12-10 at 18:30 in response to the Riverbend Chemical Warehouse Fire and subsequent evacuation order for the 2-mile radius downwind from the incident. As of the 20:30 headcount, we are sheltering 186 evacuees, including approximately 54 children under 12, 9 infants under 1 year old, and 27 individuals over age 70. We have also received 4 wheelchair users and 3 individuals who self-identify with significant mobility limitations.

RESOURCE STATUS (CURRENT ON-HAND):
• General cots: 150 total received; 146 currently in use; 4 held for medical isolation or emergency intake.
• Blankets: 190 standard Red Cross-style blankets; most are in use with only 7 remaining unused.
• Pediatric/infant sleeping supplies: 3 portable cribs/pack-and-plays; 2 infant-safe bassinets; limited supply of fitted sheets.
• Hygiene kits: 180 basic kits (adult-oriented); we have improvised 12 child kits by repackaging supplies.
• ADA/Access needs: 3 low-height cots suitable for mobility-impaired evacuees; all 3 currently assigned.

ISSUES / CONCERNS:
1) OVERCAPACITY ON COTS: Based on current evacuee trends and the evening intake pattern, we anticipate an additional 40–60 evacuees overnight, primarily family units arriving after work hours and individuals relocated from the temporary staging area at the community center. We are already using classroom floors and gym mats as overflow, which is not ideal for older adults, people with back issues, or families with small children.

2) INSUFFICIENT BLANKETS: With the outside temperature dropping to the low 30s (F) and the gym HVAC system struggling to maintain consistent warmth in the far corners of the room, we are double-layering blankets for older adults and young children, which significantly reduces our overall blanket availability. Several evacuees arrived with only light clothing, having evacuated quickly due to the shelter-in-place and subsequent evacuation orders.

3) LIMITED PEDIATRIC & INFANT SUPPLIES: We have more infants and very young children than anticipated. We are currently:
   • Doubling up siblings on cots with rolled blankets as barriers, which is not ideal from a safety standpoint.
   • Using extra standard pillows and folded blankets as makeshift bumpers for toddlers.
   • Reusing a limited number of waterproof pads, which raises hygiene concerns.
Parents have specifically requested safer sleep options for infants (e.g., firm, enclosed sleeping spaces) and more child-appropriate blankets that are smaller and lighter.

4) ANTICIPATED DURATION: Per the last briefing from Incident Command, the evacuation order may remain in place for at least another 24–48 hours pending air quality testing and structural assessment of the warehouse. This means that the current improvisations for sleeping arrangements will likely extend into multiple nights unless additional supplies are brought in.

REQUESTED RESOURCES (PRIORITIZED):
1) COTS
   • 60 additional standard adult cots.
   • 10 low-height or ADA-accessible cots (sturdy, easier for older adults and mobility-limited evacuees to transfer on/off).
   Rationale: This quantity would allow us to:
      – Move all evacuees off the floor and onto cots.
      – Maintain a small buffer (approx. 10–15 cots) for late arrivals, medical isolation, and potential overnight transfers from other shelters.

2) BLANKETS
   • 120 additional standard blankets (adult size).
   • 40 child-sized or lightweight blankets (for infants and children under 10).
   Rationale: We aim to ensure at least two blankets per person in the gym and classroom sleeping areas, given the drafty conditions and the number of older adults and young children. The child-sized blankets are important because standard blankets are too heavy and bulky for infants and toddlers and pose a safety risk when used incorrectly.

3) PEDIATRIC & INFANT SLEEPING SUPPLIES
   • 8 portable cribs/pack-and-plays with firm mattresses.
   • 10 infant-safe sleep surfaces (e.g., bassinets or Moses-basket style units) suitable for newborns to 6 months.
   • 30 waterproof mattress pads (crib or cot size) with 2–3 fitted sheets per unit.
   Rationale: We currently have 9 infants and expect at least 3 more based on family information from the staging area. We need separate, safe sleeping spaces for each infant, plus a small buffer for late arrivals. Waterproof pads and multiple fitted sheets are critical for hygiene when dealing with frequent spills, diaper leaks, and illness.

4) HYGIENE & COMFORT (SECONDARY PRIORITY BUT IMPORTANT):
   • 40 pediatric hygiene kits (child-sized toothbrushes, mild toothpaste, baby wipes, small combs, etc.).
   • 5 large bottles of fragrance-free, sensitive-skin baby wash and shampoo.
   • 10 tubs of diaper rash cream.
   These items are being requested in coordination with our on-site volunteer pediatric nurse and the Medical Unit. Parents have expressed concern about maintaining basic hygiene for their children over multiple days.

DELIVERY & LOGISTICS:
• Preferred delivery window: As soon as practical, ideally between 22:30 tonight (2025-12-11) and 02:00 tomorrow (2025-12-12), to allow setup before the early-morning hours when temperatures drop further and additional arrivals are expected.
• Delivery location: Riverbend High School, 1420 Willow Creek Rd, Riverbend, OH 44127. Main entrance on the north side (off Willow Creek Rd). Use the bus loop for unloading.
• Check-in procedure: Upon arrival, please check in with Shelter Operations at the main lobby registration table. Ask for Daniel Alvarez or the current Shelter Supervisor on duty.
• Offloading support: We have a volunteer team of 6–8 individuals available to assist with unloading and staging supplies in the auxiliary gym.

SPECIAL CONSIDERATIONS:
• We have three evacuees who use wheelchairs and one who uses a walker. ADA-accessible cots are a high priority for them, as they are currently using standard-height cots that are difficult to transfer to and from.
• One family with a 3-week-old infant is currently improvising sleep by using a standard cot with rolled towels; they have expressed significant anxiety about the infant rolling or getting trapped in bedding. A proper bassinet or infant-safe sleep surface would directly address this concern.
• Several older adults have complained of hip and back pain after sleeping one night on thin mats. Additional cots and blankets would allow us to pad sleeping surfaces more effectively and reduce discomfort.

CONTACT FOR FOLLOW-UP:
Daniel Alvarez, Shelter Operations Unit Leader
Riverbend Emergency Management Agency
Cell: (216) 555-3498
Email: dalvarez.ops@riverbendoh.gov
If possible, please confirm estimated time of arrival (ETA) for requested supplies via text or email so we can plan staff coverage and setup accordingly.

reply.reply:
RESOURCE ORDER STATUS – RIVERBEND HIGH SCHOOL SHELTER

Reference: Request from Daniel Alvarez dated 2025-12-11 21:35 regarding additional cots, blankets, and pediatric supplies.

ACTION TAKEN:
1) COTS
   • 60 standard cots and 10 low-height/ADA-accessible cots have been released from the county logistics cache located at the Riverbend Public Works Warehouse (3100 Industrial Pkwy, Riverbend, OH 44122).
   • Transport is being provided by two county box trucks (Truck 4 and Truck 7). Loading began at approximately 21:55.

2) BLANKETS
   • 120 standard blankets and 40 child-sized blankets have been allocated from the Red Cross partner cache.
   • These items are being loaded onto the same trucks referenced above.

3) PEDIATRIC & INFANT SLEEPING SUPPLIES
   • 8 portable cribs/pack-and-plays and 6 infant bassinets are available in the county health department cache.
   • We were only able to locate 6 bassinets tonight; an additional 4 are on order from a regional partner but will not arrive until late tomorrow.
   • 24 waterproof pads and 40 fitted sheets (crib size) are being sent on the first shipment. Remaining pads/sheets will be delivered tomorrow as they become available.

4) HYGIENE & COMFORT ITEMS
   • 30 pediatric hygiene kits are being deployed immediately from the volunteer reception center supply room.
   • 5 large bottles of fragrance-free baby wash and 10 tubs of diaper rash cream have been confirmed and added to the shipment.
   • The remaining 10 pediatric hygiene kits will be assembled by volunteers overnight and delivered on the second run tomorrow morning.

ESTIMATED DELIVERY TIME (ETA):
• First shipment (cots, blankets, initial pediatric supplies) departed the warehouse at approximately 22:20.
• Estimated arrival at Riverbend High School shelter: between 22:40 and 22:55, depending on traffic and road closures near the incident perimeter.

DELIVERY INSTRUCTIONS:
• Drivers have been instructed to report to the main entrance bus loop on the north side of the school as requested.
• They have been given your name and role (Daniel Alvarez, Shelter Operations Unit Leader) as the primary point of contact.
• If you are not available, they will ask for the current Shelter Supervisor and the Logistics Liaison if present.

FOLLOW-UP & REMAINING GAPS:
• We acknowledge the shortfall of 4 infant bassinets and a portion of the waterproof pads/fitted sheets. These items are being prioritized in tomorrow’s 08:00 regional resource coordination call.
• Please provide a quick headcount update and any changes in demographic needs (e.g., additional infants, older adults, or mobility-impaired evacuees) by 06:00 tomorrow so we can adjust the second shipment accordingly.

POINT OF CONTACT – LOGISTICS:
Karen Mitchell, Logistics Section Chief
Riverbend Incident Command Post – Riverbend Civic Center
Cell: (216) 555-9824
Email: kmitchell.logistics@riverbendoh.gov

If conditions at the shelter change significantly overnight (e.g., large influx of evacuees, HVAC failure, or medical surge), call or text me directly so we can escalate to regional mutual aid and adjust resource priorities.
